%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 5.6 Frequency Response from Pole-Zero Locations

> **Section Objective:** Predict a discrete-time system's frequency response directly from the pole-zero plot of \(H[z]\).

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Factored transfer function
- Unit-circle evaluation
- Pole-zero distance rule
- Pole-zero angle rule
- Peak-and-notch recognition


## 1. Factored form shows the geometry

Each **numerator factor** \((z - z_i)\) defines a directed vector pointing from zero \(z_i\) to the evaluation point \(z\). Each **denominator factor** \((z - \gamma_i)\) defines a vector from pole \(\gamma_i\) to \(z\).

**Symbol guide:**
- \(b_0\) — overall gain constant
- \(z_i\) — location of the \(i\)-th zero in the complex z-plane
- \(\gamma_i\) — location of the \(i\)-th pole in the complex z-plane
- \(N\) — number of poles (and zeros)

**Minimal example:** If the numerator contains \((z - 0.8)\), there is a zero at \(z = 0.8\) on the real axis.

#### Exam Trigger
A problem gives a pole-zero plot or a factored \(H[z]\) — use this form directly.

#### Common Misuse
Do not treat \(z_i\) or \(\gamma_i\) as frequencies. They are fixed locations in the complex z-plane, not frequency values.

$$H[z]=b_0\frac{(z-z_1)(z-z_2)\cdots(z-z_N)}{(z-\gamma_1)(z-\gamma_2)\cdots(z-\gamma_N)}$$

## 2. Frequency response means walking around the unit circle

To find the frequency response \(H[e^{j\Omega}]\), substitute \(z = e^{j\Omega}\) into \(H[z]\). Because \(|e^{j\Omega}| = 1\) for all \(\Omega\), this evaluation point always lies on the **unit circle** — changing \(\Omega\) rotates the point around the circle without changing its radius.

\(\Omega\) is the **discrete-time radian frequency**, measured in radians per sample.

**Minimal examples:**
- \(\Omega = 0\) → evaluation point is \(z = 1\) (rightmost point)
- \(\Omega = \pi\) → evaluation point is \(z = -1\) (leftmost point)

#### Exam Trigger
Phrases like "sketch the frequency response from the pole-zero plot" always mean: sweep \(e^{j\Omega}\) around the unit circle.

#### Common Misuse
Do not plug in an arbitrary \(z\) value. Frequency response uses the unit circle only.

$$z = e^{j\Omega}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Magnitude is a product of zero distances divided by pole distances

**Formula (5.41)** — This is the core geometric rule for reading magnitude from a pole-zero plot.

**Symbol guide:**
- \(r_i = |e^{j\Omega} - z_i|\) — distance from zero \(z_i\) to the unit-circle evaluation point
- \(d_i = |e^{j\Omega} - \gamma_i|\) — distance from pole \(\gamma_i\) to that same point
- Both distances change as \(\Omega\) sweeps around the unit circle

**When to use it:** Estimating or sketching the amplitude response from a pole-zero plot without computing \(H[z]\) algebraically.

**Minimal example — notch:** If the evaluation point moves very close to a zero, one \(r_i\) becomes nearly zero. The numerator product collapses, so \(|H[e^{j\Omega}]|\) has a deep notch at that frequency.

#### Exam Trigger
"Which frequency has the largest magnitude?" — find the unit-circle point closest to a pole.

#### Common Misuse
Students often reverse the rule. Zeros are in the **numerator** — a nearby zero drives magnitude **down**. Poles are in the **denominator** — a nearby pole drives magnitude **up**.

$$|H[e^{j\Omega}]| = b_0 \frac{r_1 r_2 \cdots r_N}{d_1 d_2 \cdots d_N}$$

## 4. Phase is zero angles minus pole angles

The phase response is built from the **angles of the directed vectors** from each pole and zero to the evaluation point \(e^{j\Omega}\).

- \(\angle(e^{j\Omega} - z_i)\) — angle of the vector pointing **from zero \(z_i\) to the unit-circle point**
- \(\angle(e^{j\Omega} - \gamma_i)\) — angle of the vector pointing **from pole \(\gamma_i\) to the unit-circle point**
- Zero-vector angles are **added**; pole-vector angles are **subtracted**
- If \(b_0\) is a positive real number, then \(\angle b_0 = 0\) and it contributes nothing to the phase

**Near-miss to avoid:** A student who measures all angles from the **origin** is wrong. Each angle belongs to its own vector drawn from a specific pole or zero to \(e^{j\Omega}\), not from the origin.

#### Exam Trigger
Phase sketching questions, or "which pole-zero plot gives a given phase trend?" — apply this formula by tracing how each vector angle changes as \(\Omega\) increases.

$$\angle H[e^{j\Omega}] = \angle b_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \angle(e^{j\Omega} - z_i) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \angle(e^{j\Omega} - \gamma_i)$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Factored form \(H[z]=b_0\frac{(z-z_1)\cdots(z-z_N)}{(z-\gamma_1)\cdots(z-\gamma_N)}\) places zeros in the numerator and poles in the denominator.
- Frequency response uses \(z=e^{j\Omega}\): sweep the unit circle; \(|e^{j\Omega}|=1\) always.
- Magnitude rule (5.41): \(|H[e^{j\Omega}]|=b_0\frac{r_1\cdots r_N}{d_1\cdots d_N}\) — nearby zero → notch; nearby pole → peak.
- Phase rule: \(\angle H[e^{j\Omega}]=\angle b_0+\sum\angle(e^{j\Omega}-z_i)-\sum\angle(e^{j\Omega}-\gamma_i)\) — add zero angles, subtract pole angles.

*Next, you will use this geometry to sketch and interpret real filter responses faster.*
